past_medical_history: Hypertension, diagnosed 8 years ago, managed with medications
Recurrent transient ischemic attacks suspected 6 months ago, evaluated at outside hospital
Peripheral vascular disease with bilateral lower extremity claudication
Iron deficiency anemia, treated intermittently with oral iron supplementation
Remote history of rheumatic fever in childhood
family_history: Father died at age 52 of sudden cardiac death while working in the fields
Mother has hypertension and diabetes, alive at 78
Older brother had a pacemaker implanted at age 55 for 'slow heart rhythm'
Paternal grandmother reportedly had recurrent fainting spells

social_history.tobacco: Former smoker, 15 pack-year history, quit 3 years ago
social_history.occupation: Small business owner, runs a grocery store
social_history.living_situation: Lives with wife and two adult children in a two-story home
social_history.exercise: Previously walked 2 km daily but has significantly curtailed activity over the past 4 months due to fatigue and lightheadedness

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Glasgow Coma Scale 15. Speech fluent without dysarthria or aphasia. Attention intact; able to spell WORLD backwards. Recall 3/3 objects at 5 minutes. No confusion at time of evaluation, approximately 30 minutes after the syncopal episode.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual fields full to confrontation bilaterally. Pupils equal, round, reactive to light 3mm to 2mm bilaterally. Fundoscopic exam shows mild arteriolar narrowing consistent with chronic hypertension; no papilledema, hemorrhages, or exudates. CN III/IV/VI: Extraocular movements intact without nystagmus. CN V: Sensation intact to light touch in all three divisions. Masseter strength symmetric. CN VII: Face symmetric at rest and with movement. CN VIII: Hearing grossly intact bilaterally. CN IX/X: Palate elevates symmetrically, gag intact. CN XI: Trapezius and sternocleidomastoid strength 5/5 bilaterally. CN XII: Tongue midline without fasciculations.
neurological_exam.motor: Bulk mildly decreased in bilateral lower extremities. Tone normal throughout, no spasticity or rigidity. Strength 5/5 in all upper extremity muscle groups bilaterally. Lower extremities: hip flexors 5/5 bilaterally, knee extensors 5/5 bilaterally, ankle dorsiflexion 5/5 bilaterally, plantar flexion 5/5 bilaterally. No pronator drift.
neurological_exam.sensory: Light touch and pinprick intact throughout. Vibration sense mildly diminished at bilateral great toes. Proprioception intact at great toes bilaterally.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally, triceps 2+ bilaterally, brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally, patellar 2+ bilaterally, Achilles 1+ bilaterally. Plantar responses flexor (downgoing) bilaterally. No clonus.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose intact bilaterally without dysmetria. Heel-to-shin normal bilaterally. Rapid alternating movements intact.
neurological_exam.gait: Steady gait with normal base. Tandem gait intact. Romberg negative.
neurological_exam.additional: No tongue laceration or lateral tongue bite marks. No urinary or fecal incontinence during the episode. Small bruise on right temple from fall. Bilateral dorsalis pedis pulses diminished. Orthostatic vitals: supine BP 138/82, HR 48; standing at 1 min BP 126/78, HR 52; standing at 3 min BP 118/74, HR 54 with mild lightheadedness reported.

patient.chief_complaint: Sudden loss of consciousness with fall and brief convulsive movements
patient.history_present_illness: Mr. Rajesh Khatri is a 58-year-old man with a history of hypertension, peripheral vascular disease, suspected prior transient ischemic attacks, and iron deficiency anemia who presents to the emergency department after a witnessed syncopal episode that occurred approximately 45 minutes ago at his grocery store. According to his employee who witnessed the event, the patient was standing behind the counter when he suddenly became pale, his eyes rolled upward, and he collapsed to the floor striking his right temple on the edge of the counter. The witness reports that after falling, the patient exhibited brief bilateral arm and leg jerking lasting approximately 8-10 seconds, followed by a period of limpness. He regained consciousness within approximately 20-30 seconds and was able to converse coherently almost immediately, asking what had happened. He denies any warning symptoms such as lightheadedness, visual changes, chest pain, or palpitations preceding the event. He did not bite his tongue, and there was no urinary incontinence. This is the third such episode in approximately two months. The first episode occurred six weeks ago while he was climbing stairs at home; he suddenly lost consciousness without warning, fell backwards, and was found by his wife at the bottom of three stairs. She reports he regained awareness within seconds and did not appear confused. The second episode occurred three weeks ago while seated at his desk; he slumped forward and his son noted brief arm jerking for a few seconds before he woke up and was immediately oriented. After the second episode, he visited a local clinic where he was told these might be seizures related to his previous cerebrovascular issues, and was referred for neurological evaluation. He has not yet had an EEG or MRI performed. Over the past four months, he has noticed progressive fatigue, exertional dyspnea with activities such as climbing a single flight of stairs or walking more than a short distance, and occasional lightheadedness upon standing quickly. He has also felt occasional episodes of his heart seeming to pause briefly, though he has never measured his pulse during these episodes. He denies fever, recent illness, chest pain, orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, or peripheral edema. His wife notes that he has seemed increasingly tired and has been taking afternoon naps, which is unusual for him.

background.posterior_dominant_rhythm: 9.5 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric bilaterally, well-modulated, reactive to eye opening
background.anterior_rhythms: Low to medium amplitude beta activity, symmetric, without focal slowing
background.sleep_features: Brief drowsiness captured with vertex sharp transients and K-complexes; no epileptiform discharges during drowsiness

artifacts.description: Intermittent low-amplitude muscle artifact, does not obscure underlying cerebral activity

activating_procedures.hyperventilation: Performed for 3 minutes. Symmetric bilateral slowing observed, returning to baseline within 20 seconds of cessation. No epileptiform discharges provoked.
activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Performed at flash frequencies 1-30 Hz. Normal photic driving response at 14-20 Hz. No photoparoxysmal response elicited.

eeg.impression: Normal awake and drowsy EEG. No epileptiform abnormalities identified. No electrographic seizures captured during this 40-minute routine recording. A normal interictal EEG does not exclude epilepsy; however, given the clinical semiology described (no postictal confusion, no tongue biting, immediate recovery), non-epileptic etiologies for transient loss of consciousness should be strongly considered.
eeg.limitations: Routine 40-minute recording. No clinical events captured during monitoring. Interictal sensitivity of a single routine EEG is approximately 50%.
recommended_actions: Consider cardiac monitoring to evaluate for arrhythmic causes of transient loss of consciousness
If epilepsy remains in the differential, prolonged ambulatory or video-EEG monitoring may be pursued

ecg.rhythm: Sinus bradycardia with Mobitz type II second-degree AV block

intervals.QRS: 142 ms (widened, right bundle branch block morphology)
intervals.QTc: 492 ms (borderline prolonged, partially attributable to wide QRS)

findings: Sinus bradycardia at 46 bpm
Mobitz type II second-degree AV block: intermittent dropped QRS complexes without progressive PR prolongation, 3:2 conduction pattern observed
Right bundle branch block (rsR' in V1-V2, wide slurred S wave in leads I and V6)
Left anterior fascicular block (left axis deviation, rS pattern in leads II, III, aVF)
Borderline QTc prolongation at 492 ms
Low-voltage QRS complexes in limb leads
Old Q waves in leads V1-V3, consistent with prior anteroseptal infarction versus septal fibrosis
No acute ST elevation or depression
ecg.interpretation: Sinus bradycardia with Mobitz type II second-degree AV block occurring in the setting of bifascicular block (RBBB and left anterior fascicular block). This represents advanced His-Purkinje conduction system disease with a high risk of progression to complete heart block. The intermittent dropped beats, combined with the underlying conduction abnormalities, are highly consistent with an arrhythmic mechanism for recurrent syncope. The old anteroseptal Q waves suggest prior myocardial injury that may have contributed to conduction system fibrosis.
ecg.clinical_correlation: In a patient with recurrent abrupt-onset syncope without prodrome and rapid recovery, this ECG pattern of Mobitz type II AV block with bifascicular block is strongly suggestive of intermittent high-grade AV block as the cause. Continuous telemetry monitoring and urgent cardiology consultation are recommended. Temporary transvenous pacing should be considered given the high-risk conduction pattern.

CBC.clinical_significance: Moderate anemia; may exacerbate symptoms of cardiac insufficiency and contribute to fatigue and exercise intolerance
Microcytic, consistent with iron deficiency anemia
Elevated, consistent with iron deficiency anemia

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, may reflect mild prerenal azotemia from low cardiac output or dehydration

Cardiac.clinical_significance: At the upper limit of normal; no definitive evidence of acute myocardial injury
Elevated, suggesting underlying cardiac dysfunction or volume overload; correlate with echocardiographic assessment

Thyroid.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, suggesting subclinical hypothyroidism; may contribute to bradycardia and fatigue
Low-normal free T4 with elevated TSH consistent with subclinical hypothyroidism

labs.interpretation: Moderate microcytic anemia consistent with iron deficiency (low MCV, MCH, serum iron, ferritin; elevated TIBC and RDW). Mildly elevated BUN may reflect prerenal azotemia. Elevated BNP at 289 pg/mL suggests underlying cardiac dysfunction. Troponin I at the upper limit of normal without definitive acute injury. Mildly elevated TSH with low-normal free T4 consistent with subclinical hypothyroidism, which may be a confounding factor for the bradycardia. Electrolytes and glucose normal, ruling out metabolic causes of syncope. Coagulation studies normal.

Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Moderately reduced systolic function, consistent with ischemic cardiomyopathy
Consistent with prior anteroseptal infarction territory

Echocardiogram.value: Mild mitral regurgitation, mild aortic regurgitation, mild tricuspid regurgitation
Echocardiogram.clinical_significance: Mild multivalvular regurgitation; the mitral regurgitation may be functional related to LV dilatation

Echocardiogram.value: Normal RV size and systolic function, TAPSE 20 mm

output.interpretation: Transthoracic echocardiogram reveals moderately reduced left ventricular systolic function with an ejection fraction of 38%. Regional wall motion abnormalities in the anteroseptal and apical segments are consistent with prior ischemic injury. Mild LV dilatation is present. Mild multivalvular regurgitation (mitral, aortic, and tricuspid) is noted. Right ventricular size and function are normal. These findings support underlying ischemic cardiomyopathy as a substrate for both the conduction system disease and the patient's elevated BNP.
abnormal_values_summary: Mild mitral, aortic, and tricuspid regurgitation

output.rhythm: Predominantly sinus bradycardia with multiple episodes of Mobitz type II second-degree AV block and two episodes of high-grade AV block with ventricular pauses
findings: Total recording duration: 24 hours
Predominant rhythm: sinus bradycardia with average rate 48 bpm
Mobitz type II second-degree AV block: 47 episodes observed, predominantly nocturnal
High-grade AV block (3:1 and 4:1 conduction): 2 episodes at 03:14 AM (4.2-second pause) and 05:47 AM (3.8-second pause)
No sustained ventricular tachycardia
Rare premature ventricular complexes (34 total, 0 couplets)
No supraventricular tachycardia
Heart rate variability reduced
Patient diary: lightheadedness and near-syncope at 03:15 AM correlating with the 4.2-second pause
output.interpretation: 24-hour Holter monitoring demonstrates significant conduction system disease with frequent Mobitz type II second-degree AV block and two episodes of high-grade AV block producing ventricular pauses of 4.2 and 3.8 seconds. The longest pause correlates with patient-reported symptoms of lightheadedness and near-syncope. These findings confirm intermittent high-grade AV block as the mechanism for this patient's recurrent syncopal episodes and represent a Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: The documented symptomatic high-grade AV block with prolonged ventricular pauses confirms the diagnosis of cardiac syncope due to advanced conduction system disease. Urgent cardiology consultation for permanent pacemaker implantation is indicated.

Tilt_Table_Test.clinical_significance: Chronotropic incompetence; inadequate heart rate response to orthostatic challenge

Tilt_Table_Test.value: Test terminated early at 22 minutes due to development of Mobitz type II block on continuous ECG monitoring with 3.6-second pause
Tilt_Table_Test.clinical_significance: Spontaneous AV block provoked by orthostatic stress; confirms cardiac arrhythmic mechanism

output.interpretation: Tilt table test was terminated early at 22 minutes due to the development of Mobitz type II AV block with a 3.6-second ventricular pause during upright tilt. No typical vasovagal response pattern was observed. The provocation of AV block during tilt supports an arrhythmic (cardiac) mechanism for syncope rather than a neurocardiogenic mechanism. Baseline chronotropic incompetence was also noted.
abnormal_values_summary: Mobitz type II AV block with 3.6-second pause provoked during tilt

findings.location: Anteroseptal and apical segments of the left ventricle

signal_characteristics.late_gadolinium_enhancement: Subendocardial to transmural late gadolinium enhancement in the LAD territory (anteroseptal and apical segments)
signal_characteristics.T1_mapping: Elevated native T1 values in the septum (1120 ms, normal <1050 ms)

additional_observations: No evidence of infiltrative cardiomyopathy (amyloid, sarcoid)
output.impression: Cardiac MRI demonstrates ischemic cardiomyopathy with moderately reduced LV systolic function (LVEF 36%), LV dilatation, and subendocardial to transmural late gadolinium enhancement in the LAD territory consistent with prior myocardial infarction. Elevated native T1 values in the septum suggest additional diffuse interstitial fibrosis. No evidence of infiltrative cardiomyopathy or active inflammation. The pattern of fibrosis involving the interventricular septum is consistent with the substrate for the conduction system disease observed on ECG and Holter monitoring.
recommended_actions: Correlate with coronary angiography to assess current coronary anatomy
Consider ICD versus pacemaker given reduced LVEF and arrhythmic risk

output.query: syncope cardiac conduction disease Mobitz type II AV block pacemaker indication

results.title: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay
results.journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
results.key_finding: Permanent pacemaker implantation is a Class I recommendation for patients with second-degree Mobitz type II AV block, regardless of symptoms, due to high risk of progression to complete heart block.

results.title: European Society of Cardiology Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope (2018)
results.key_finding: Syncope in the presence of bifascicular block with intermittent Mobitz type II or higher-degree AV block is considered diagnostic of cardiac syncope. Pacemaker implantation is recommended without additional electrophysiology testing.

results.title: Natural history of chronic second-degree atrioventricular block
results.key_finding: Mobitz type II AV block carries a significantly higher risk of progression to complete heart block compared to Mobitz type I, with 5-year progression rates exceeding 40% even in initially asymptomatic patients.

output.summary: Current ACC/AHA/HRS and ESC guidelines recommend permanent pacemaker implantation as a Class I indication for Mobitz type II second-degree AV block, particularly when associated with symptoms such as syncope. In the setting of bifascicular block with documented Mobitz type II block and syncope, the mechanism is considered confirmed and electrophysiology study is not required prior to pacemaker implantation. Mobitz type II block carries a high natural history risk of progression to complete heart block.

output.proposed: Atenolol 50 mg daily in a patient with Mobitz type II AV block
interactions: Atenolol (beta-blocker) suppresses AV nodal and infranodal conduction, potentially exacerbating Mobitz type II AV block and increasing risk of higher-grade block
Amlodipine (calcium channel blocker - dihydropyridine) has minimal AV nodal effects but combined with beta-blocker may contribute to bradycardia
contraindications: Beta-blockers are relatively contraindicated in patients with Mobitz type II or higher-degree AV block without a functioning pacemaker
Atenolol should be discontinued or held in this clinical context pending pacemaker implantation
warnings: Abrupt discontinuation of beta-blockers may cause rebound tachycardia and hypertension; if discontinuing, taper gradually unless high-grade block is immediately threatening
After pacemaker implantation, beta-blocker therapy may be safely resumed if indicated for heart failure (switch to carvedilol or bisoprolol with LVEF 38%)
output.formulary_status: Atenolol available on formulary; however, in the context of reduced LVEF, evidence-based beta-blockers (carvedilol, bisoprolol, metoprolol succinate) are preferred
alternatives: After pacemaker: carvedilol 3.125 mg twice daily titrated to target dose (evidence-based for HFrEF)
After pacemaker: bisoprolol 1.25 mg daily titrated to target dose

output.rhythm: Unable to complete standard exercise stress test protocol
findings: Test terminated at Stage 1 of Bruce protocol (3 minutes) due to development of 2:1 AV block with symptomatic presyncope
Baseline: sinus bradycardia with Mobitz type II AV block
During exercise: worsening of AV block from 3:2 to 2:1 conduction ratio
Marked chronotropic incompetence: peak HR 72 bpm (39% of age-predicted maximum)
Blood pressure response: appropriate initial rise from 136/80 to 148/84, then declined to 112/68 at termination
No significant ST-segment changes beyond baseline RBBB-related changes
Recovery: return to baseline conduction pattern within 5 minutes
output.interpretation: Exercise stress test severely limited by exercise-induced worsening of AV block. The deterioration from Mobitz type II to 2:1 AV block with exercise is highly pathological and indicates infranodal (His-Purkinje) conduction disease, as opposed to AV nodal block which typically improves with exercise and sympathetic stimulation. This finding strongly supports the need for permanent pacemaker implantation.
output.clinical_correlation: Exercise-induced worsening of AV block confirms infranodal conduction disease and is an additional Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation. The severe chronotropic incompetence explains the patient's progressive exercise intolerance and exertional dyspnea.

output.query: subclinical hypothyroidism bradycardia AV block conduction disease

results.title: Thyroid Dysfunction and Cardiac Arrhythmias
results.key_finding: Overt hypothyroidism can cause sinus bradycardia and rarely first-degree AV block, but is not a recognized cause of Mobitz type II or higher-degree AV block. Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 4.5-10 with normal free T4) is unlikely to cause significant conduction system disease.

results.title: Cardiovascular manifestations of subclinical hypothyroidism
results.journal: Journal of Clinical Endocrinology and Metabolism
results.key_finding: Subclinical hypothyroidism may contribute to mild sinus bradycardia and diastolic dysfunction but does not typically cause infranodal conduction defects such as bundle branch block or Mobitz type II AV block.

output.summary: While subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 6.8 with normal free T4) may contribute to sinus bradycardia, it does not explain the Mobitz type II AV block or bifascicular block pattern, which indicate structural His-Purkinje disease. Thyroid function should be optimized with levothyroxine, but this does not obviate the need for pacemaker implantation for the documented high-grade conduction disease.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Cardiac syncope due to Mobitz type II second-degree atrioventricular block with bifascicular block (advanced His-Purkinje conduction system disease)

differential.key_distinguishing: Normal EEG, no postictal confusion beyond 1 minute, no tongue biting, no incontinence, immediate lucid recovery — all argue strongly against epilepsy. Brief convulsive movements are consistent with convulsive syncope (cerebral hypoperfusion-induced myoclonus) rather than true seizure.

No clear triggers (pain, prolonged standing, emotional stress), no prodromal symptoms (nausea, warmth, diaphoresis), episodes occur in various positions including seated, and age/sex profile is less typical for vasovagal syncope.

Mild orthostatic changes noted on exam, but episodes also occur while seated and without positional component. Atenolol and anemia may contribute to orthostatic changes, but the ECG findings of Mobitz type II block better explain the abrupt syncope.

differential.diagnosis: Subclinical hypothyroidism causing bradycardia and syncope
differential.key_distinguishing: Mildly elevated TSH with normal free T4 may contribute to bradycardia but does not explain Mobitz type II AV block or bifascicular block, which require structural conduction system disease.

Moderate iron deficiency anemia (Hgb 10.8) may contribute to fatigue and exercise intolerance but alone does not explain abrupt loss of consciousness without prodrome. However, anemia may lower the hemodynamic threshold for symptoms during bradycardic episodes.

optimal_actions.action: Obtain 12-lead ECG to evaluate cardiac rhythm and conduction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB), sinus bradycardia

optimal_actions.action: Obtain routine EEG to rule out epilepsy given convulsive movements during episodes
Order comprehensive laboratory panel including CBC, BMP, troponin, BNP, and thyroid function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Microcytic anemia, elevated BNP, subclinical hypothyroidism — confounders identified but not sufficient to explain the presentation

optimal_actions.action: Request continuous cardiac monitoring (Holter) to document intermittent AV block and pauses
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Frequent Mobitz type II block with episodes of high-grade AV block and prolonged pauses correlating with symptoms

optimal_actions.action: Request transthoracic echocardiogram to assess cardiac structure and function
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Reduced LVEF, regional wall motion abnormalities consistent with ischemic cardiomyopathy

optimal_actions.action: Check drug interactions for atenolol in the setting of AV block
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Atenolol contraindicated in Mobitz type II block without pacemaker

optimal_actions.action: Search literature for guidelines on pacemaker indications in Mobitz type II AV block with syncope
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Class I indication for permanent pacemaker implantation

optimal_actions.action: Initiate continuous telemetry, hold atenolol, consult cardiology urgently for pacemaker evaluation, and consider temporary pacing if symptomatic pauses recur

critical_actions: Recognize ECG pattern of Mobitz type II AV block with bifascicular block as high-risk for progression to complete heart block
Identify that brief convulsive movements represent convulsive syncope (cerebral hypoperfusion) rather than epileptic seizures
Discontinue or hold atenolol which may worsen AV block
Urgent cardiology referral for permanent pacemaker implantation
Recognize that subclinical hypothyroidism and iron deficiency anemia are confounders but do not explain the high-grade conduction disease
contraindicated_actions: Starting antiepileptic drugs without confirmed epilepsy diagnosis — the convulsive movements are from cerebral hypoperfusion, not seizures
Increasing atenolol dose for rate control — beta-blockers are contraindicated in Mobitz type II block without a pacemaker
Administering IV verapamil or diltiazem — non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers will further impair AV conduction
Attributing syncope solely to iron deficiency anemia or subclinical hypothyroidism and discharging without cardiac workup
Performing lumbar puncture — not indicated in this clinical scenario
key_reasoning_points: The abrupt onset of syncope without prodrome, brief duration of unconsciousness, and immediate return to full orientation favor a cardiac etiology over epilepsy or vasovagal syncope
Brief convulsive movements lasting 8-10 seconds during syncope are a well-recognized phenomenon (convulsive syncope) caused by transient cerebral hypoperfusion and do not indicate epilepsy

The ECG finding of Mobitz type II second-degree AV block with bifascicular block (RBBB + LAFB) identifies advanced His-Purkinje conduction system disease with high risk of intermittent complete heart block
A normal EEG, while not excluding epilepsy, supports the clinical impression that these episodes are not seizures when combined with the characteristic semiology

Subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 6.8) and atenolol use are confounding factors that may contribute to bradycardia but do not explain the Mobitz type II block pattern, which requires structural conduction system disease
Iron deficiency anemia (Hgb 10.8) may lower the symptomatic threshold for hemodynamic compromise during bradycardic episodes but is not the primary cause of syncope
The family history of sudden cardiac death and pacemaker implantation in first-degree relatives raises suspicion for familial conduction system disease
The elevated BNP and subsequent echocardiographic finding of reduced LVEF suggest underlying ischemic cardiomyopathy as the substrate for the conduction disease

metadata.difficulty_description: Moderate difficulty: The presentation mimics seizures due to convulsive syncope, and confounding findings of subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 6.8), iron deficiency anemia, and beta-blocker use create plausible alternative explanations for the bradycardia and symptoms. The ECG shows Mobitz type II block with bifascicular block rather than the more obvious complete heart block, requiring the agent to synthesize the clinical and ECG findings.
